comments: From a collection of 274 tapes mixing John Peel shows, Friday Rock Shows and Alan Freeman Saturday Shows from 1978 to 1983. The Derby Box was acquired by the John Peel Yahoo Group.

track_listing.track: 'Kid Charlemagne' (2xLP-Greatest Hits 1972-1978) (ABC Records). Originally on (LP-The Royal Scam) (ABC Records).
Wish I Were Born In Beverly Hills

track_listing.details: LP-A Trick Of The Tail; Charisma

track_listing.track: Set The Controls For The Heart Of The Sun
New Town Animal In A Furnished Cage
'Don't Think Twice, It's Alright' (LP-Bob Dylan At Budokan) (CBS)
'It's Only Rock N Roll (But I Like It)' (2xLP-Love You Live) (Rolling Stones Records)
'Strange Magic' (3xLP-Three Light Years) (United Artists). Originally on (LP-Face The Music) (Jet)

transcript.text: On 275 and 285 and stereo VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Hi there, this is Tommy Vance. Welcome to the Friday Rock Show.
Right, on tonight's program we have sessions by XTC and one by Robin Trough from February 1975.
We've got classic tracks from The Floyd, Steely Dan, Genesis and The Stones, just to name a few.
We've got new cuts from Alice Cooper, Bob Dylan, Public Image, Kiss and Champion.
There's a Friday Night Connection, a new one put together maybe by you, and also the answer to last week's set.
But to start off, here's something by Ted Nugent.
And Fagin, and some of the greatest session men in the United States of America, there.
Kid Charlemagne, that comes from the LP Steely Dan's Greatest Hits.
It's a double album, out right now on ABC.
Before that, you heard Stormtrooping, from the LP Double Live Gonzo, by Ted Nugent, out right now on Epic.
And we played it first up because Martin Wasey of 109 New Road in Chingford told us to do exactly that.
Now, here's our first session track from XTC.
XTC from their latest session that was called Mechanic Dancing.
Way back in February 1975 in the BBC studios, Mr. Robin Trower sounded like this.
That's called It's Only Money. It was by Robin Trower.
You'll find it on the LP for Earth Below.
Robin Trower and XTC are our session groups tonight, both put together in the studio.
one in 1975 one in 1978 by tony wilson there's a new band kicking around rather they're called

they changed shape with the seasons.
And this weekend, Bob Kilby tries to keep them still for long enough
to present a current picture of the Albertos and their new venture, Skite.
Rock On even ventures into the area of Dr. Funkenstein
with a preview of Parliament Funkadelic.
That's Rock On for this weekend, then.
The Cars, The Albertos, and Parliament Funkadelic, all on Rock On.
Saturday, 4.31, Rock On.
Thank you, Stuart.
All four members of KISS, that amazing, painted American group, have brought out solo albums.
We're going to play you a couple of tracks, one by Gene Simmons and one by Paul Stanley.
Starting off with Gene.
From KISS, from his solo album, the track was called Tonight You Belong to Me,
and it's on Casablanca, as indeed, of course,
is the solo album by the gentleman called Gene Simmons.
His track was entitled Radioactive.

all on Casablanca, and if you buy all four,
you get four bits of paper that, when you put them all together,
makes a really big, beautiful poster.
What a merchandising job.

which is very, very expensive.

and they said they are going to put it out in this country,
but they don't exactly know when,
so maybe you'd be advised to sort of hang around for a while.
£16.95 is a lot of money for a double album,
but it has been shipped all the way from Japan, I guess.
we prized that copy from the hands of one John Peel.
Didn't give him any money for it.
He was very generous.
Peely, on Monday, between 10 o'clock and 12,
is going to be featuring the Buzzcocks
and a new group to radio by the name of Cure.
Radio One!

Lots and lots of mail,
and thank you very much for writing in.

NN8, 3JR.

Write to me. I'm Tommy Vance.
Friday Night Connection, BBC, Radio 1, London, W1A4, WW.
And you could win yourself a £10 record token.
And if you've just this moment come in from the boozer
and you missed the answer to last week's Friday Night Connection,
it was Mr Bob Dylan.
Do put your address on the cards.
Don't remain anonymous.
Rather like somebody from Sheffield did.

The Rolling Stones from Love You Live, and it's only rock and roll, but I like it.

Jackie, hope you like that.
Before that, I played something by Public Image,
featuring, of course, Johnny Rotten from their first album.
That, indeed, is the single. It's got the same title, Public Image.
I once did a two-hour radio programme with Johnny Rotten
in which he chose all the music,
and his musical taste is incredibly varied and really entertaining.

That was one of his favourite tracks.

which, incidentally, you will find on the LP, Go To.
Somewhere in the back of your mind,
there must be a piece of music that means something
that you haven't heard for quite some time.
Now, the BBC have very kindly given me the run of the whole of their library,
so the odds are that it'll be in that library.
And if you write to me and let me know what it is,
I'll dig it out and play it for you.
It goes like this.
Tommy Vance, Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, London, 4,

W1A 4WW.

and I'll go into the library,
and I'll dig it out.

or rather, we played Strange Magic from Three Light Years,
which is a box set that's on the market at the moment for Christmas.
It includes the three albums by the ELO,
On the Third Day, El Dorado, and Face the Music.
It's very beautifully packaged,
and I would suggest that you possibly contemplate
giving one to your granny for Christmas.
Robin Traor guitar, James Doar bass and vocals,
and Bill Lorden on the drums, as they sounded in February 1975.
All the cuts that we played by them tonight
can be found on the LP for Earth Below.
Great session, produced by, well, none other than Tony Wilson.
The album is called Twelve, the band of Artley James Harvest.
That's their latest album.
The track you just heard was called Turning in Circles.
The last track from the excellent XTC, and that was called The Rhythm.
You'll find it on the LP Go To.
I'd like to play this next record for a mate of mine with whom I had a drink at lunchtime,
Alan Freeman, who sends his best wishes.
Also for a lady by the name of Alice Hutton down there in Bath.
Life on the Line, which is out on Ireland.
Do anything that you want to do.
And that was the Friday Night Rock Show.
I hope you enjoyed the sessions by XTC and also by Robin Traer.
Our engineers were David and Mark,
and the whole shebang was produced by Tony Wilson.
Peely on Monday night has got the cure
for the first time ever on radio, and also the buzzcocks.
I'm Tommy Vance, here in London.
Wherever you are, I trust you have a really wonderful weekend.
I'll see you next Friday at 10 o'clock.
Till then, take care.
